coredata.dc:title: A portable high-performance self-insulated solar evaporator based on wooden sponge for seawater desalination and wastewater purification
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Xi
Chen, Yingying
Lin, Pengcheng
Su, Hua
Chen, Ying
coredata.dc:description: Over 70 % of the earth's surface is water, though people are facing a shortage of clean water. Desalination and purification plants have been utilizing inexhaustible saline and dirty water for decades, but here we present a portable solar-powered device that works during the journey. The independent steam generator is based on a novel wooden material coated with carbonized carbon dots (CCD). A facile hydrothermal treatment is applied to the balsa wood to produce a superhydrophilic, salt-resisting, and self-insulated wooden sponge. The CCD microspheres show broad absorption over the solar spectrum while the wooden sponge provides a rapid water supply and heat localization. The floating CCD@wooden sponge evaporator generates steam at the rate of 2.24 kg m−2 h−1 and has an energy efficiency of 85.0 % under 1 sun irradiation. With a unique design of portable water purifier, a traveler could easily obtain clean water after collecting and purifying undrinkable water such as seawater and wastewater on the move as long as the sun shines.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbonized carbon dot
Wooden sponge
Solar-driven evaporation
Seawater desalination
Wastewater purification

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Assembly of the solar-driven portable dual-purpose purifier

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Solar-driven portable dual-purpose purifier. (a) Synthesis of the CCD spheres as the solar-thermal evaporator. (b) The illustration of the CCD@wooden sponge evaporator. (c) Preparation of 2D layered super-hydrophilic wooden sponge through hydrothermal treatment on balsa wood. (d) The purification process and the components of the solar-driven portable dual-purpose purifier.

Characterization of solar-thermal materials. (a) Light yellow CD powder, (b,c) the TEM images of CD, and (d) the contact angle of CD. (e) Black CCD powder, (f,g) the SEM images of CCD, and (h) the contact angle of CCD. (i) XPS survey spectrum of CD. (j) XPS survey spectrum of CCD. (k) Direct normal irradiance of the sun (AM 1.5) and UV–vis-NIR absorption spectra of CD and CCD. (l) IR thermogram of CCD under 1 sun.

Structure and hydrophilicity of wooden sponge. (a)Original woodblock and wooden sponge with a darker color. (b) Cross section of an original woodblock. (c) Cross section of a wooden sponge. (d) Vertical section of an original woodblock. (e) Vertical section of a wooden sponge. (f) EDS mapping of original woodblock and (g) wooden sponge. The SEM images of CCD. (h) The contact angle of original wood over time. (i) Contact angle of wooden sponge over time. (j) Dyed water transport experiment and (k) the vertical section of a woodblock at the final state. (l) Thermal conductivities of core and surface layer of wooden sponge, and original wood.

Performance and structure of CCD@wooden sponge evaporator. (a) Mass loss of water samples with a series of CCD@wooden sponge evaporators. (b) The evaporation rate of evaporators with different amounts of CCD coated on the surface. (c) The energy efficiency of evaporators with different amounts of CCD coated on the surface. (d) This work's optimum energy efficiency and evaporation rate under 1 sun compared with prior research based on wooden materials. [17,19,20,23,32–40]. (e) The photograph and infrared thermogram of CCD@wooden sponge evaporator under 1 sun. (f) A 6-hour salt resistance test on the evaporator. (g) The salinity of four artificial seawater before and after desalination test. (h) Cycling test on CCD@wooden sponge evaporator. (i) The indigo dye colloid as artificial wastewater before and after purification by solar-thermal evaporation. (j) UV–Vis absorption spectra of artificial wastewater, purified water, and several standard solutions of indigo dye colloid.

Portable clean water production: solar desalination and purification test on real seawater and kitchen wastewater. (a) Collecting water from the water source. (b-e) Clean water production at a static state, on the back, across the shoulder, and with a backpack. (f) Clean water harvesting with the conical cap dismantled. (g) Mass loss, sunlight irradiance, and mass of clean water during desalination of seawater. (h) Mass loss, sunlight irradiance, and mass of clean water during purification of kitchen wastewater. (i) Concentration of 5 main elements of seawater before and after desalination. (j) Photograph of kitchen wastewater and purified water. (k) Total organic carbon (TOC) of kitchen wastewater and purified water.

head.ce:title: A portable high-performance self-insulated solar evaporator based on wooden sponge for seawater desalination and wastewater purification

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory on Functional Soft Condensed Matter, School of Materials and Energy, Guangdong University of Technology, Guangdong 510006, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory on Functional Soft Condensed Matter

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Guangdong Provincial Key Laboratory on Functional Soft Condensed Matter, School of Materials and Energy, Guangdong University of Technology Guangdong 510006, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Over 70 % of the earth's surface is water, though people are facing a shortage of clean water. Desalination and purification plants have been utilizing inexhaustible saline and dirty water for decades, but here we present a portable solar-powered device that works during the journey. The independent steam generator is based on a novel wooden material coated with carbonized carbon dots (CCD). A facile hydrothermal treatment is applied to the balsa wood to produce a superhydrophilic, salt-resisting, and self-insulated wooden sponge. The CCD microspheres show broad absorption over the solar spectrum while the wooden sponge provides a rapid water supply and heat localization. The floating CCD@wooden sponge evaporator generates steam at the rate of 2.24 kg m−2 h−1 and has an energy efficiency of 85.0 % under 1 sun irradiation. With a unique design of portable water purifier, a traveler could easily obtain clean water after collecting and purifying undrinkable water such as seawater and wastewater on the move as long as the sun shines.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Shortage of clean water is inevitable for one civilization, which would experience a population boom, development of industries, natural hazards, and wars. Since the natural water sources are not sufficient, the utilization of seawater and wastewater has been a focus of researchers for centuries. Diverse techniques were developed and widely applied such as distillation, [1] electrodialysis, [2] and reverse osmosis. [3] Traditional desalination and purification methods are high energy-consuming, indicating pollution-intensive power generation and considerable cost. Nevertheless, clean solar energy remains substantial and mostly unexploited while polluting fossil fuels are facing an increasing risk of depletion. In face of crises of water resources, energy, and pollution, solar-thermal desalination and purification are proposed. [4,5]

The essential component of a solar-thermal desalinator or purifier is solar-thermal conversion material, which harvests sunlight and converts light into heat. Thus, solar-thermal material (STM) comes to the focus of attention. The STMs are high in light absorbance and solar-thermal energy conversion efficiency across the solar spectrum. According to the level of heat localization, the STM could be dispersed in the water, attached to the bottom, and floated on the surface. Considerable heat loss to the bulk water is observed except for the floating design, which is inspiring to this work.

Over the past few years, nano semiconductors, [6] metals, [7,8] polymers, [9–11] carbon materials [12–14], and MXenes [15] have been reported as efficient STM. Typically, the desired STM should have properties including high solar absorbance, high energy conversion efficiency, and chemical stability. However, the fabrication of solar-thermal semiconductors (including MXene) is harsh and costly. [16] As for metals, the cost of noble metals is hardly acceptable for mass freshwater production, much less the secondary pollution of heavy metals. [17] The utilization of nano-sized or micro-sized polymers would inevitably worsen microplastic pollution. Among all kinds of STM, carbon materials are the most low-cost, environmentally friendly, and applicable for mass production.

Along with the STM, a water transport material (WTM) is needed to connect the water supply from the bulk water to the STM. In addition to the hydrophilic property, floatation and heat-insulation are preferred due to the promising heat loss reduction. [18] Hydrogels such as polyvinyl alcohol and starch are widely reported as excellent WTM, though they are rather fragile. However, natural WTM such as woods, features excellent mechanical strength, high production, and reasonable cost. So far, the evaporation rates of evaporators based on raw woods [19,20] are lower than those of hydrogel-based evaporators [21,22] while sharing the same STM, indicating insufficient water supply instead of energy supply. Besides, natural woods are unable to avoid salt deposition unless being drilled, [23,24] or the lignin within is removed. [25,26]

Herein, by taking advantage of the high specific surface area, low toxicity, and excellent chemical stability of CD. A bowling-ball-like CCD was prepared by carbonizing CD powder under N2 flow (Fig. 1a). However, the time cost and yield of CDs had been unattractive until Li et al. developed a kilogram-scale synthesis method of CD [27,28]. Nevertheless, the fluorescent property of CD is a drawback for an STM due to the energy loss. In this research, the CDs are carbonized to promote agglomeration and remove surficial groups to eliminate the fluorescent property.

In the aspect of WTM, the super hydrophilicity and softness of a typical hydrogel are desired, hence the hydrophobic and rigid lignin within the wood should be removed. A hydrothermal treatment with strong aqueous solution of NaOH was applied to balsa wood. With the degradation of lignin, the 1D water channels are expanded to a 2D layered structure, leading to salt resistance due to salt exchange [23] (Fig. 1c). The nature of the short-time hydrothermal process with strong alkali is a vigorous treatment to the superficial layer of wood. A noticeable difference in hydrophilicity leads to air chambers inside the fully wetted WTM, which is low thermal conducting. The difference in thermal conductivity between the superficial and the core layer of a hydrothermally treated woodblock (named wooden sponge) indicates a self-insulation property. This could reduce heat loss [29] and increase energy efficiency. The synergetic effect of solar energy harvest and water transport brings forth an evaporator with an evaporation rate of 2.24 kg m−2 h−1 and an energy efficiency of 85.0 % under 1 sun irradiation. No salt deposit was observed during a 24-hour evaporation test, indicating excellent salt resistance.

A portable dual-purpose solar-thermal water purification device consisting of a large CCD@wooden sponge evaporator (Fig. 1b) can desalinate and purify on the move. Five main elements (Na, K, Mg, Ca, B) in seawater are 99 % rejected while the total organic carbon of kitchen wastewater is reduced by 96.4 %. The CCD@wooden sponge evaporator is fixed in a portable dual-purpose purifier illustrated in Fig. 1d. The shell consists of two concentric cylinders made of polymethyl methacrylate, whereas the inner one with CCD@wooden sponge floating inside is shorter. An operator could feed the water tank (the inner cylinder) by simply throwing the purifier into the water source and pulling it up with straps while the water inlet is blocked by water pressure. A transparent cone made of polycarbonate sheet covers the outer cylinder to receive the vaper. Drips of condensed clean water on the internal surfaces are collected between two cylinders which could be released at any time. The whole process does not require a static condition, indicating the capability of working on the move.

The major novelties of this work can be summarized as follows.
(1)
The novel wooden sponge removes the bottleneck of solar-driven evaporator based on wooden materials due to rapid water transport, salt resistance and self-heat-insulation.
(2)
An amorphous carbon material was prepared through the calcination of carbon dots. It has broad absorption of sunlight and reduced hydrophilicity.
(3)
A portable dual-purpose purifier was built. The purifier is available to remove salt and involatile organics from the water while traveling.
NaOH (AR, Damao Chemical Reagent Factory), acetaldehyde solution (AR, 40 %, Damao Chemical Reagent Factory), hydrochloric acid (AR, 36.0 % ~ 38.0 %).

The carbon dots are prepared as the method reported by Li et al. [27] NaOH (150 g) was carefully added to 500 mL of acetaldehyde solution while stirring, and the reaction was finished after stirring for 2 h. The dark brown muddy crude product was slowly moved to 1.2 mol L−1 hydrochloric acid and washed three times with an intense stir. For each time the solid was ground before washing. Then the product was washed with deionized water to neutrality. The synthesis of carbon dots was completed after the solid was dried at 70 °C in a blast oven. The carbon dots are carbonized at 400 °C with N2 flow for 1 h. A black char was produced.

A balsa wood block (3 cm × 3 cm × 4 cm) and 75 mL 2 mol L−1 were heated to 160 °C in a stainless autoclave for 5 h. The treated wood was ultrasonically washed with DI water. The wooden block was freeze-dried to prepare a superhydrophilic elastic wooden sponge.

CCD was ultrasonically dispersed in 5 mL ethanol as an unstable colloid. The colloid was uniformly coated on the upper surface of a wooden sponge, which was vertically placed on bibulous paper. The evaporator was cut into a cylinder that is 2 cm in diameter and 3 cm in height.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assembly of the solar-driven portable dual-purpose purifier
ce:section.ce:para: The upper half of a traditional polymethyl methacrylate (PMMA) water sampler was cut away, which was intended for the water input. The modified water sampler was embedded into the bottom of a larger open PMMA cylindrical container. The interspace was the container of the condensed purified water. A PP tube followed by a rubber tube was planted through the wall near the bottom of the cylindrical container as the water output. The open container was capped by a polycarbonate (PC) cone.

The morphologies and microstructures were observed through SEM (Hitachi, S-3400 N) and TEM (JEOL, JEM-2100). The absorbance of CD and CCD powders was converted from reflectance spectra recorded on Ultraviolet-Visible-Near-Infrared (UV–Vis-NIR) spectrophotometer (SHIMADZU, UV-3600 Plus). The absorption spectra of liquid samples were captured by a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Shanghai Precision Instrument, UV-8000 s). Infrared thermographs were captured by a thermal camera (FLIR, A325sc). Fourier Transform Infrared (FTIR) spectra were captured by Nicolet iS50R Research FTIR Spectrometer. The concentrations of Na, Mg, K, Ca and B in water samples were calculated from the emission spectra of the elements recorded by ICP-MS (Agilent 7700 ICP-MS). The element composition of the CP microspheres was confirmed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra recorded on Thermo Fisher, Escalab 250Xi.

The seawater desalination and wastewater purification were carried out outdoors or under artificial sunlight (CEAULIGHT, CEL-HXUV300 xenon lamp and AM1.5 optical filter). The average light intensity on the sample surface was 1 kW m−2, which was measured by a light intensity meter (CEAULIGHT, CEL-NP2000). The coefficient of thermal conductivity was measured with Hot Disk TPS 500 Thermal Constants Analyser. The upper surface of the evaporators was exposed to the air during evaporation tests. Then the mass loss of water samples was measured by a balance (Puchun, 1000/0.01) every 5 min for at least 1 h of steady operation. Considering the changing condition of the weather and atmosphere, the desalinated and purified water samples were produced indoors under artificial sunlight with closed glassware (Fig. S1). Four square evaporators (2 cm × 2 cm × 3 cm) are contained by a two-centimeter-thick EPE ring.

In CCD, a carbonized derivative of the mass-produced CD, was fabricated as a solar-thermal conversion material. CCD was expected to retain the high specific surface area of CD, which would present efficient photothermal conversion.

CD was synthesized as a massive bright yellow powder (Fig. 2a). It is easily confirmed through TEM (Fig. 2b,c) that the diameter of CD particles is 2–4 nm with a lattice distance of 0.22 nm, which aligns with the reported data. [27] As shown in SEM images (Fig. S2), the diameter of CD is observed as grape-like agglomerative clusters while the black CCD powder (Fig. 2e) is bowling-ball-like porous spheres with a diameter of about 2 μm (Fig. 2f,g). The original CD is hydrophilic due to their CO bonds and -OH groups, presenting a low contact angle of 37.7° (Fig. 2d). The hydrophilic groups are massively removed during carbonization, thus the contact angle of CCD increased up to 68.6° (Fig. 2h), which is helpful to the evaporation. [30]

The chemical modification from CD to CCD is characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The survey spectrum of CD (Fig. 2i) and CCD (Fig. 2j) interprets that the major elements of CD are C and O (C:O = 4.86) while the O element within CCD is significantly reduced (C:O = 20.0). Apart from the hydrocarbon signal at 284.8 eV, high-resolution spectra (Fig. S3a,b) for C1s of CD show CO and CO peaks centered at 288.7 eV and 286.4 eV, respectively. CD may contain hydroxyl, carbonyl, carboxyl, and formyl, nevertheless, no peak is found at 287.9 eV, indicating the nonexistence of ketone. The high-resolution O1s peaks (Fig. S3c,d) are fitted, which reveals the molar fraction of hydroxyl, carboxyl and formyl are 19.2 %, 25.3 %, and 55.5 %, respectively. The oxygenous groups are dramatically reduced, among which a small amount of carboxyl is reserved. Besides, the Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum of CD (Fig. S4) shows broad absorption signals at 3350 cm−1, corresponding to the OH stretching vibration with hydrogen bonds. The peak at 1716 cm−1 indicates CO stretching vibration. The above signals assigned to oxygen-containing groups died out after carbonization, while the CH stretching vibrations (peaks at 2954 cm−1, 2918 cm−1, and 2861 cm−1) and CH bending vibration (peaks at 1441 cm−1 and 1375 cm−1) remain strong. The FTIR spectrum of CCD presents immensely decreased peaks at 3350 cm−1 and 1716 cm−1, interpreting again that the hydroxyl had been mostly removed during carbonization.

Raman peaks (Fig. S5) at 1357 cm−1 and 1601 cm−1 are attributed to the D band and G band, respectively, while the D band indicates the defects or disorder of sp2 carbon, and the G band indicates the graphite structure. The integrated intensity ratio of D and G peak (ID/IG) is calculated as 2.66, indicating that the amorphous carbon constitutes the majority of the CCD spheres. It has been widely reported that the amorphous carbons have broad absorption, [31] which is an essential property of an STM.

The absorption of CD and CCD across the solar spectrum is recorded by UV–Vis-NIR spectroscopy (Fig. 2k). The absorbance from 220 to 2500 nm wavelength of CCD exhibits constantly high absorbance, which is 93.9 % in total (see the calculation of total solar absorption of CD and CCD in Supplementary Information), throughout the solar spectrum after carbonization. As a result, the maximum average temperature of a CCD sheet could be heated to 56.0 °C within 3 min under 1 sun simulation (Fig. 2l), implying CCD microspheres preferred material for solar energy utilization.

Along with the darkened color (see Fig. 3a) and removal of lignin through hydrothermal treatment with a strong alkali, the 1D vessels as water channels within natural wood (Fig. 3b,d) are expanded to 2D layered structure (Fig. 3c,e). The broadened water channel enables the convection of water, preventing the accumulation of salt on the surface. Both wood and wooden sponge contain C and O elements, analysis of EDS mappings (see Fig. 3f,g) captured from both materials showed that the OC ratio of the original wood is 0.31, which is boosted to 1.3 by hydrothermal treatment. The sharp increase of oxygenous groups enables sufficient water supply through the wooden sponge. It is recorded that the contact angle of original balsa wood and treated wooden sponge are both 0° (Fig. 3h,i).

Water within a hydrated wooden sponge is easily squeezed out and absorbed, indicating a much higher water transport rate than untreated wood (Fig. S6). To prove the assumption, a dyed water transport experiment is carried out (Fig. S7). It took 9 min for the wooden sponge to fully wet the upper surface of itself while the original wood failed at 30 min. At the end of the test mentioned above, the all-in-red wooden sponge (Fig. 3j) is halved longitudinally (Fig. 3k), revealing that the core of a wooden sponge is far less dyed, in other words, less hydrophilic than the surface layer. The difference is attributed to the nature of the hydrothermal process. It is possible that the hydrothermal treatment could give the wooden sponge a self-insulation structure since the core is observed to be much less humid than the surface layer, leading to lower thermal conductivity. Then the vertical thermal conductivities of thoroughly wetted original wood, the core, and the surface layer of the wooden sponge are measured as 0.239 W m−1 K−1, 0.315 W m−1 K−1, and 0.409 W m−1 K−1. The thermal conductivity of the surface layer is at least 29.8 % higher than that of the core, which confirmed the self-insulated structure of wooden sponge.

A series of evaporators with different amounts of CCD particles coated on the wooden sponge are prepared to explore the optimum coating. Their surfaces are observed through SEM and the evaporation rates are tested to explain the reason why the evaporation rate rises and falls.

The CCD@wooden sponge evaporators are exposed to 1 sun irradiation in a pure water evaporation test. The mass loss over time, which is corresponding to the mass of vaporized water, is recorded (Fig. 4a). The evaporation rate grew rapidly until the CCD content reached 10 mg cm−2, where the evaporation rate stays around 2.24 kg m−2 h−1 (Fig. 4b). As the content of CCD is added beyond 25 mg cm−2, the evaporation rate slowly dropped to 2.00 kg m−2 h−1. Energy efficiency is calculated (see the calculation of the enthalpy of vaporization and energy efficiency in Supplementary Information for details) with Eq. (1).
(1)
η
=
mH /
C Opt
P 0
in which m and H represent the evaporation rate and the real vaporization enthalpy of water, respectively. P
0 is 1 sun irradiance (1 kW m−2), and C
Opt is optical concentration. Calculation suggests that the average energy efficiency is 85.0 % (Fig. 4c). Here we compare the CCD@wooden sponge evaporator with recent research based on wood (Fig. 4d). On one hand, the CCD@wooden sponge evaporator has moderate energy efficiency. On the other hand, the evaporation rate is relatively high in all research However, the interaction between the CCD and the wooden sponge is still unclear. It is significant to analyze why CCD could be coated firmly on the wooden sponge, and how the loading content of CCD acts on the evaporation rate. It is observed through the SEM that the agglomerated CCD particles are fixed between the cracks and channels on the surface of evaporators. (Fig. S8a) The evaporator with 15 mg cm−2 CCD on the surface (Fig. S8b) showed multiple blocked channels. When the CCD content increased up to 30 mg cm−2 (Fig. S8c), only a few channels are left unblocked. As for the evaporator with 40 mg cm−2 CCD (Fig. S8d), there are only large cracks on the surface with no available water channels observed. In conclusion, although CCD plays a crucial role in the solar-thermal process, enough clear water channels are indispensable. CCD@wooden sponge evaporator with 15 mg cm−2 CCD is chosen for further tests due to consideration of both performance and economics. The infrared thermogram (Fig. 4e) of this evaporator is captured under 1 sun. The average temperature of the upper surface is stabilized at 40.0 °C after 10 min.

Accumulation of salt crystals is common in solar-thermal desalination, which would severely undermine the solar energy absorption and evaporation rate. The salt resistance test was carried out with a salt-covered CCD@wooden sponge evaporator floating on artificial average seawater. (Fig. 4f) The salt on the surface was gradually transported back to the bulk water in 6 h, indicating the excellent salt resistance of the evaporator. Among the evaporators mentioned in Fig. 4d, 46 % are salt-resistant, which means no salt deposit is observed during long-term evaporation. 23 % of the evaporators are not salt-resistant, and the salt resistance of the rest is unclarified. A range of artificial seawater with different concentrations that 0.8 wt% (Baltic Sea), 3.5 wt% (average seawater), 4.0 wt% (Red Sea), and saturated solution are prepared to test the desalination performance with a simple device (Fig. S1). The concentration of Na+ before and after desalination is measured by inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS). The spectra indicate that the Na+ concentration of each desalinated sample meets the standard of the World Health Organization (WHO) (Fig. 4g). The stability of the evaporation was examined through a cycling test, including 10 cycles in which the evaporator was placed under 1 sun irradiation for 40 min followed by 20-minute darkness (Fig. 4h). The mass loss was recorded and its derivative was taken as the evaporation rate. It is observed that there is no notable decline in the evaporation rate. A long-term evaporation test on the CCD@wooden sponge evaporator was carried out. The evaporator was irradiated by 1 sun simulation for 25 h while vaporizing average seawater (3.5 wt%), which is replenished from time to time. The evaporation rate was tested every 3 h while the photographs were taken every hour. The upper surface of the evaporator remained unheated in the first hour, leading to a low average evaporation rate. The evaporation rate fell from 2.07 kg h−1 m−2 to 1.90 kg h−1 m−2 due to the increasing concentration of salt (Fig. S9). However, no salt crystal was observed during the test (Fig. S10), indicating excellent salt resistance of the CCD@wooden sponge evaporator. Samples of the lowest part of the evaporator after the long-term evaporation test and a new evaporator was washed carefully with DI water. The mass of both samples is recorded before and after freeze-drying. It is calculated that the mass of water within the sample of the new evaporator was 95.3 % while it is 94.4 % within the long-tested evaporator. No significant decrease in swelling property is observed after the long-term evaporation test. Vertical slices of both the new and the long-tested evaporators, which are the walls of channels, are sampled and fully wetted. The stress-strain curves of both samples are shown in Fig. S11. The tensile strength of the new CCD@wood evaporator is 0.047 MPa while that of the evaporator after long-term test is 0.030 MPa. The tensile strength was reduced by 35.7 %.

The purification performance of the CCD@wooden sponge evaporator for organic waste is also found excellent. The solar-thermal purification process with the evaporator produced completely transparent clean water (Fig. 4i), which is spectrally close to DI water (Fig. 4j). The concentration of the indigo blue in dyed aqueous solution can be reduced from 9.74 × 103 mg L−1 to 43.0 mg L−1 based on the calibration curve (Fig. S12). Over 99.6 % of indigo blue is removed in this test.

Practical production of clean water with real seawater and kitchen wastewater is examined outdoors, where there is unstable temperature, air pressure, and sunlight irradiance. Fig. 5a-f shows how the portable dual-purpose purifier works from water collection to clean water production. The device is equipped with straps, which not only enable facile water collection from a water source (Fig. 5a) but also make it possible to carry out solar-thermal water production while traveling (Fig. 5b-e). Clean water could be extracted by releasing the water stopper, and the conical cap could be easily lifted while cleaning (Fig. 5f). The solar evaporation-collection experiments are conducted under natural sunlight (9:00–17:00) with a portable dual-purpose purifier. The time-dependent mass change and sunlight irradiance are monitored (Fig. 5g,h). The total mass loss of seawater and desalinated water during this daytime test are measured to be 5.12 kg m−2 and 0.75 kg m−2, respectively. The concentrations of all the five primary elements (Na, Mg, K, Ca, and B) are significantly reduced below the WHO standard of drinkable water after solar desalination (Fig. 5i). The total evaporation and collection in the purification test are measured to be 7.13 kg m−2 and 2.38 kg m−2, respectively. The total organic carbon (TOC) of the kitchen wastewater sample after solar-thermal purification presented a decline of 96.4 % (see Fig. 5j,k).

In summary, a sort of porous carbon sphere is obtained through carbonizing CD. The evaporator was prepared after the CCD are coated on the surface of wood which was hydrothermally treated with a strong alkali. As a new solar-thermal conversion material, the CCD spheres show an excellent absorbance of 93.9 % across the solar spectrum. Hydrothermal treatment with strong alkali enabled the self-insulated property in addition to enhanced hydrophilicity of balsa wood. The evaporation rate is about 2.24 kg m−2 h−1 as CCD content lies between 10 and 25 mg cm−2. The portable solar-thermal water purification device, which incorporates a CCD@wooden sponge evaporator, provides desalinated water that meets the WHO standards of drinking water and purified water with low TOC levels.

Xi Zhao: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Visualization. Yingying Chen: Investigation, Validation. Pengcheng Lin: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization, Funding acquisition. Hua Su: Investigation. Ying Chen: Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. U20A20299), Guangzhou Science and Technology Planning Project (No. 202103000042), Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation (2019A1515011379), Guangdong Special Support Program (2017TX04N371).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.desal.2023.116549.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Photothermal membrane distillation for seawater desalination
Adv. Mater.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: [1] A. Politano, P. Argurio, G. Di Profio, V. Sanna, A. Cupolillo, S. Chakraborty, H.A. Arafat, E. Curcio, Photothermal membrane distillation for seawater desalination, Adv. Mater., 29 (2017) 1603504.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrodialysis desalination for water and wastewater: a review
Chem. Eng. J.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: [2] S. Al-Amshawee, M.Y.B.M. Yunus, A.A.M. Azoddein, D.G. Hassell, I.H. Dakhil, H.A. Hasan, Electrodialysis desalination for water and wastewater: A review, Chem. Eng. J., 380 (2020) 122231.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Reverse osmosis desalination: a state-of-the-art review
